title: OT Quotations That Don't Match
question: Why do NT authors sometimes quote the OT differently than our Hebrew text reads?

responses.summary: Keener emphasizes that the Septuagint (LXX) was the Bible of the early church. Greek-speaking Jews and Christians read, memorized, and cited from the LXX as their authoritative Scripture, just as English-speaking Christians today cite from the NIV, ESV, or KJV. When NT authors quote the OT in forms that differ from the Hebrew Masoretic Text, they are usually following the LXX — the Greek translation produced in the 3rd–2nd centuries BC. In many cases (like Hebrews 10:5 / Psalm 40:6), the LXX reflects a different Hebrew source text (Vorlage) that may predate the Masoretic Text. The Dead Sea Scrolls have confirmed that the pre-Masoretic Hebrew textual tradition was more diverse than previously thought, and the LXX often preserves readings that are older than the Masoretic standardization. The NT authors were not being careless; they were using the authoritative translation available to them.
responses.strengths: Grounded in the historical reality of LXX usage in the early church. Dead Sea Scrolls evidence confirms the existence of diverse Hebrew text forms. Explains the majority of OT/NT divergences straightforwardly.
responses.weaknesses: Not all NT divergences from the Hebrew can be explained by the LXX — some quotations match neither the LXX nor the Masoretic Text. Does not address cases where NT authors appear to quote from memory or paraphrase freely.

responses.summary: Marcus and other scholars situate the NT's use of the OT within the broader context of Second Temple Jewish interpretive methods. Pesher interpretation (from the Qumran scrolls) applied prophetic texts directly to the interpreter's own community as the 'true' fulfillment: 'This is that which was spoken' — a formula virtually identical to Peter's speech in Acts 2:16. Midrashic exposition freely combined, rearranged, and adapted scriptural texts to address new situations, often linking passages by shared vocabulary (gezerah shavah) or thematic analogy. Paul's extended argument in Galatians 3–4 and Matthew's formula quotations both employ these recognized Jewish methods. The NT authors were not violating their own community's standards of citation; they were employing the full toolkit of contemporary Jewish biblical interpretation, which prioritized theological meaning and communal application over verbatim reproduction.
responses.strengths: Grounded in documented Second Temple Jewish practice (Dead Sea Scrolls, rabbinic literature). Explains the most creative and unexpected NT uses of the OT. Avoids anachronistically imposing modern citation standards.
responses.weaknesses: Some conservative evangelicals are uncomfortable with the comparison to pesher/midrash, fearing it reduces NT quotations to arbitrary proof-texting. The line between legitimate Jewish interpretive method and eisegesis can be difficult to draw.

responses.summary: Calvin understood the NT's use of the OT through the lens of Christ as the interpretive key to all of Scripture. When Matthew applies Hosea 11:1 ('Out of Egypt I called my son') to Jesus, he is not claiming that Hosea was making a prediction about the Messiah in the modern sense. Rather, he is reading the entire OT narrative typologically: Israel is the 'son' who was called out of Egypt, and Jesus is the true Son who recapitulates and fulfills Israel's story. Calvin saw this Christological re-reading as legitimate because Christ is the telos to which all Scripture points (Luke 24:27, 44). The apparent 'misquotations' are actually instances of the NT authors reading Scripture at a deeper level than its original historical referent — seeing in it patterns, types, and trajectories that find their completion in Christ. This is not arbitrary but follows from the conviction that the same God speaks throughout Scripture.
responses.strengths: Provides a coherent theological framework for the NT's creative use of the OT. Rooted in Jesus' own hermeneutic as presented in Luke 24. Has deep roots in patristic and Reformed exegesis.
responses.weaknesses: Can appear circular: the NT use of the OT is legitimate because Christ fulfills the OT, and we know this because of the NT's use of the OT. Does not address cases where the divergence is purely textual rather than interpretive.

responses.summary: Jobes's work on the Septuagint highlights a crucial fact often overlooked in the inerrancy debate: the Hebrew text was not standardized until well after the NT was written. Before the Masoretic standardization (completed by the 10th century AD, though with roots in the 1st–2nd century), multiple Hebrew text forms circulated in Jewish communities. The Dead Sea Scrolls demonstrate this dramatically — some biblical scrolls from Qumran agree with the Masoretic Text, others agree with the LXX, and still others represent a third textual tradition. When a NT quotation differs from our Masoretic Text, it may well be following a Hebrew text form that was equally authoritative in the 1st century but was not the form that survived the later standardization. This shifts the question entirely: the NT authors may not be 'misquoting' at all but citing a legitimate text that simply differs from the one that became standard.
responses.strengths: Grounded in the Dead Sea Scrolls evidence for pre-Masoretic textual plurality. Removes the assumption that the Masoretic Text is always the 'correct' original. Directly addresses the most troubling textual divergences.
responses.weaknesses: Cannot account for all divergences — some NT citations match neither the Masoretic Text, the LXX, nor any known text form. The theory works for specific cases but does not explain the full range of phenomena.

responses.summary: Longman emphasizes that the NT authors, writing under the guidance of the Holy Spirit, had a unique authority to interpret and apply the OT that goes beyond any human interpretive method. When Paul finds a Christological meaning in a passage that Hosea intended as a reference to the Exodus, or when Matthew sees a fulfillment pattern that is not immediately apparent in the original text, they are not making exegetical errors but exercising inspired interpretive authority. This does not mean their methods are arbitrary — they consistently follow recognizable patterns (typology, analogy, fulfillment) — but it does mean that their interpretive conclusions carry divine authority even when modern exegetes would not arrive at the same reading through grammatical-historical method alone. The NT authors model a way of reading the OT that is Christologically centered, theologically rich, and ultimately authorized by the Spirit who inspired both testaments.
responses.strengths: Affirms the NT authors' unique interpretive authority. Explains why the NT's use of the OT cannot simply be replicated as a modern hermeneutical method. Consistent with a high view of inspiration.
responses.weaknesses: Risks becoming a conversation-stopper that avoids engaging with the historical and textual evidence. If only inspired authors can read the OT this way, the hermeneutical model has limited applicability for the modern reader.

context: The NT contains approximately 300 direct quotations of the OT, and many of these differ — sometimes significantly — from the Hebrew text (Masoretic Text) as we have it. Some follow the Greek Septuagint (LXX) translation, which itself sometimes diverges from the Hebrew. Others appear to paraphrase freely, combine multiple OT texts, or apply passages in ways that seem disconnected from their original context. Examples include Hebrews 10:5 quoting Psalm 40:6 ('a body you prepared for me' vs. 'my ears you have opened'), Matthew 2:15 applying Hosea 11:1 to Jesus when the original refers to Israel, and the numerous 'fulfillment' citations whose connection to the OT source is not immediately obvious.

key_verses.text: Therefore, when Christ came into the world, he said: “Sacrifice and offering you did not desire, but a body you prepared for me.”
Sacrifice and offering you did not desire — but my ears you have opened — burnt offerings and sin offerings you did not require.
where he stayed until the death of Herod. And so was fulfilled what the Lord had said through the prophet: “Out of Egypt I called my son.”

consensus: Scholars broadly agree that the NT authors operated with different citation conventions than modern writers. Most evangelical and critical scholars recognize the role of the Septuagint, Jewish interpretive traditions (pesher, midrash, typology), and the early church's Christological re-reading of the OT as legitimate factors explaining the divergences. The question is not whether the NT authors cited accurately by modern standards, but whether their interpretive methods were legitimate within their own historical and theological context.

title: Significant Manuscript Variants
question: With so many variant readings in manuscripts, how can we trust the Bible's text?

responses.summary: MacArthur emphasizes the extraordinary reliability of the NT text by placing the variants in perspective. Of the approximately 138,000 words in the Greek NT, roughly 99.5% of the text is not in question. The remaining 0.5% involves variants that potentially affect meaning, and none of these touch any major doctrine. By comparison, Homer's Iliad — the next best-attested ancient text — survives in fewer than 2,000 manuscripts with a 5% variation rate. The sheer volume of NT manuscripts (5,800+ in Greek alone, plus 10,000+ Latin, plus thousands in other languages) means we can reconstruct the original text with a degree of confidence unmatched by any other document from the ancient world. The large number of variants is actually evidence of abundance, not corruption: the more manuscripts you have, the more trivial variations you will find, but the easier it becomes to identify the original reading.
responses.strengths: Effective at placing the raw variant count in meaningful context. The comparison to other ancient texts is legitimate and powerful. The abundance-produces-variants principle is sound.
responses.weaknesses: The 99.5% figure is sometimes challenged as oversimplified. Does not address specific high-profile variants (Mark 16, John 7:53–8:11, 1 John 5:7) in detail. Can minimize the complexity of actual text-critical decisions.

responses.summary: Robertson's foundational work in NT Greek textual criticism established many of the principles still used today. He demonstrated that manuscript variants fall into identifiable categories: unintentional errors (homoeoteleuton, dittography, haplography), intentional harmonizations (scribes making one Gospel account match another), theological corrections (scribes modifying theologically ambiguous passages), and liturgical additions (scribes inserting familiar worship language). Each category has predictable patterns that trained critics can recognize and evaluate. Robertson argued that text-critical methodology — comparing readings across manuscript families, evaluating internal evidence (which reading explains the origin of the others?), and assessing external evidence (which reading has the earliest and most diverse support?) — allows scholars to reconstruct the original text with high confidence. The existence of variants is not a crisis but a solvable problem, and the tools to solve it have been well-developed over two centuries of scholarship.
responses.strengths: Provides the actual methodology for evaluating variants rather than just asserting reliability. The categorization of variant types is still standard in textual criticism. Empowers informed engagement with the evidence.
responses.weaknesses: The methodology involves judgment calls, and scholars sometimes disagree on which reading is original. Some variants resist easy resolution even with sophisticated methodology. Requires technical knowledge that most lay readers lack.

responses.summary: Calvin affirmed that God's providence ensured the faithful transmission of Scripture across the centuries. This is not a claim that every manuscript is perfect or that scribes never erred, but that God superintended the process so that his Word would never be lost or fatally corrupted. Calvin noted that the very existence of multiple manuscript traditions serves providence: where one tradition has a scribal error, another preserves the original reading, so that through comparison the text can be recovered. This view does not bypass textual criticism but provides a theological framework for it: text-critical work is the means by which God's providential preservation is recognized and realized. The Reformed confessional tradition (Westminster Confession I.8) affirms that the OT in Hebrew and the NT in Greek were 'kept pure in all ages' — not in any single manuscript, but in the manuscript tradition as a whole.
responses.strengths: Provides a theological framework that affirms both God's sovereignty and the legitimacy of textual criticism. Avoids the error of equating any single manuscript or edition with the 'perfectly preserved' text. Has deep confessional roots in Reformed theology.
responses.weaknesses: Can be misused to claim perfect preservation in a specific text form (e.g., the Textus Receptus or the KJV). The concept of providence is difficult to verify empirically. Some see it as a theological assertion masquerading as a historical argument.

responses.summary: The NET Bible notes address major textual variants throughout the NT with detailed technical apparatus. Their cumulative testimony confirms what the broader discipline of textual criticism has demonstrated: the variants that meaningfully affect the text's meaning are extraordinarily few relative to the total. The NET notes categorize variants by significance — those that affect translation, those that are theologically interesting but do not change doctrine, and those that are trivial. In each case, the notes present the evidence and explain the editorial decision. Major 'test cases' like Mark 16:9–20, John 7:53–8:11, and 1 John 5:7–8 are significant not because they represent a pattern of unreliability but precisely because they are so unusual: they are the well-known exceptions in a text that is otherwise remarkably stable. The fact that scholars can identify and bracket these passages demonstrates the method's effectiveness, not the text's fragility.
responses.strengths: Engages specific variant passages rather than speaking in generalities. The 'exceptions prove the method works' argument is logically sound. Backed by detailed textual apparatus that readers can examine for themselves.
responses.weaknesses: The selection of 'significant' vs. 'insignificant' variants involves judgment. Critics like Bart Ehrman argue that the theological significance of some variants is underestimated. The reassurance may not satisfy those for whom any variant is theologically troubling.

responses.summary: Keener reframes the variant problem by comparing the NT's textual situation to other ancient documents. The NT has more manuscripts, earlier manuscripts, and manuscripts from more diverse geographic regions than any other ancient text. Most classical texts survive in fewer than 20 manuscripts, with the earliest copies dating 700–1,400 years after composition. The NT has manuscripts within decades of the originals (P52 for John, c. 125 AD; P46 for Paul, c. 200 AD). This wealth of evidence means scholars can cross-check readings across independent manuscript families in ways that are impossible for Homer, Plato, or Tacitus. Keener argues that the question should not be 'Why are there so many variants?' but 'Why do we expect perfection from manuscript transmission when we accept less from every other ancient document?' The NT's textual situation is not a weakness but a unique strength among ancient literature.
responses.strengths: Powerful comparative argument — if we trust any ancient text, we should trust the NT more. Early papyri (P52, P46, P66, P75) provide exceptional time-proximity to the originals. Accessible argument that resonates with non-specialists.
responses.weaknesses: The comparison to classical texts is somewhat asymmetrical — no one claims divine inspiration for Homer. The argument from abundance addresses reliability but not inerrancy in the strictest sense. Early papyri are fragmentary and do not cover the entire NT.

context: The NT text is preserved in approximately 5,800 Greek manuscripts, 10,000 Latin manuscripts, and thousands more in Syriac, Coptic, Armenian, and other languages. No two manuscripts agree in every detail, and scholars have identified between 300,000 and 400,000 textual variants among them. This staggering number is often cited by critics as evidence that the Bible's text is unreliable. However, the vast majority of variants are trivial (spelling differences, word order, scribal slips), and the sheer number reflects the extraordinary abundance of manuscripts rather than the corruption of the text. The question is whether the significant variants — those that affect meaning — undermine confidence in the NT text as a whole.

key_verses.text: But I tell you that anyone who is angry with a brother or sister will be subject to judgment.
Therefore, since we have been justified through faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ.

Beyond all question, the mystery from which true godliness springs is great: He appeared in the flesh, was vindicated by the Spirit, was seen by angels, was preached among the nations, was believed on in the world, was taken up in glory.

consensus: There is broad scholarly agreement — across evangelical, mainline, and critical traditions — that the NT text is the best-attested document from antiquity and that its essential message has been reliably transmitted. Scholars like Bruce Metzger, Daniel Wallace, and Bart Ehrman (despite their very different theological conclusions) agree that no cardinal doctrine of Christianity depends on a textually disputed passage. The discipline of textual criticism has produced a Greek text that scholars across traditions regard as highly reliable.
